body_txt: Whenever a character, usually while playing make-believe or Acting Out a Daydream , takes on the role of a profession that typically requires a microphone, such as a commentator, reporter, interviewer, public speaker, or singer, and they don't have access to an actual microphone, they'll create a makeshift one on the spot using whatever supplies are available. Typical non-functional examples include something microphone shaped such as a lollipop, mushroom, baseball bat, flower, hairbrush, ice cream cone, banana, small animal, fish, comb, toothbrush, cake baster, plunger, spoon, pickle, penis , etc. Of course, none of these actually produce amplified audio, unless A Wizard Did It (or the production's sound designers have hidden a wireless microphone in the prop). They are typically whatever items are found in a specific scene, such as a character in a shower singing into a hairbrush or a baker in a kitchen singing into a spoon or cake baster. A common variant seen in cartoons is an animal with a long tail, such as a cat, monkey, mouse, or lizard, speaking into the end as though it were a microphone. Due to The Coconut Effect , a fake microphone may still follow the Feedback Rule and produce an audible squeal when picked up, despite that not actually being possible if it's not connected to anything. Of course, expect the performance to conclude with a Mic Drop as a final flourish. If a makeshift microphone (well, obviously in the shape of a megaphone) is designed to produce amplified audio, that's Improvised Megaphone . Compare Everything Is an Instrument and Fantastic Light Source .
